Given some categorized constants in the:
```asp
% Define the constants in each category.
clients("Aimee"; "Ginger"; "Freda"; "Hannah").
price(150; 160; 170; 180).
masseuse("Lynda"; "Nancy"; "Teri"; "Whitney").

% Each masseuse is assigned to exactly one client and one price.
% Each client is served by exactly one masseuse and has one price.
% Each price is used exactly once.
{appointment(M, C, P) : clients(C), price(P)} = 1 :- masseuse(M).
{appointment(M, C, P) : masseuse(M), price(P)} = 1 :- clients(C).
{appointment(M, C, P) : masseuse(M), clients(C)} = 1 :- price(P).
```

```asp
% Define the constants in each category.
companies("Permias"; "Rubicorp"; "Techtrin"; "Ubersplore").
names("Cornick"; "Dreadco"; "Foltron"; "Worul").
months(1; 2; 3; 4).

% Each company is assigned exactly one rocket and one launch month.
% The predicate launch(C, N, M) means company C launches rocket N in month M.
{launch(C, N, M): names(N), months(M)}=1 :- companies(C).

% Each rocket is used exactly once.
{launch(C, N, M): companies(C), months(M)}=1 :- names(N).

% Each month is used exactly once.
{launch(C, N, M): companies(C), names(N)}=1 :- months(M).

% Clue 1: The Worul rocket launches in neither January nor February.
:- launch(C, "Worul", 1).
:- launch(C, "Worul", 2).

% Clue 2: The Dreadco rocket is made by Ubersplore.
launch("Ubersplore", "Dreadco", M) :- months(M).

% Clue 3: The rocket developed by Permias launches 1 month before the Foltron.
:- launch("Permias", N1, M1), launch("Foltron", N2, M2), M1 != M2 - 1.
```

```asp
% Define the constants in each category.
packsize(25; 30; 35; 40).
brands("Adironda"; "Grennel"; "Naturba"; "Travelore").
names("Arthur"; "Eugene"; "Natasha"; "Olga").

% Define the assignment relation: assign(Name, PackSize, Brand)
% Each name is assigned exactly one packsize and one brand.
{assign(N, S, B): packsize(S), brands(B)}=1 :- names(N).

% Constraint: Natasha is assigned to packsize S and brand "Grennel"
assign("Natasha", S, "Grennel") :- packsize(S), brands("Grennel").

% Constraint: The packsize assigned to someone is S-10 if the brand is "Grennel"
% This means: if a person is assigned brand "Grennel", then their packsize is S, and there exists someone with packsize S-10
:- assign(N, S, "Grennel"), not exists(N2, S2 : assign(N2, S2, _), S2 = S - 10).
```

```asp
% Define the constants in each category.
winners("Daniel"; "Gabriel"; "Roland"; "Vincent").
price(250; 260; 270; 280).
order(3; 4; 7; 8).

% Each winner is associated with exactly one price and one order.
{match(W, P, O): price(P), order(O)}=1 :- winners(W).

% The auction has exactly four lots, so each price, order, and winner appears exactly once.
:- #count{W, P, O : match(W, P, O)} != 4.

% Clue 1: The 7th auction lot sold for $20 less than Daniel's lot.
% So: price(7th) = price(Daniel) - 20
:- match(W, P1, 7), W != "Daniel", P1 = P2, match("Daniel", P2, _), P1 != P2 - 20.

% Clue 2: The four butterflies were: the $260 lot, the 4th lot, the 7th lot, and the one won by Roland.
% This means: the set of items includes one with price=260, one with order=4, one with order=7, one with winner=Roland.
% Since there are exactly four items, and each attribute appears once, these four must be distinct items.
% So: the item with price=260 is not the same as the one with order=4, order=7, or winner=Roland — unless they coincide.
% But the clue implies that these four are the only items, so we can deduce that:
% - The $260 lot is not the 4th lot, not the 7th lot, not won by Roland — unless one of them is the same.
% However, since the clue lists them as four distinct items, and there are only four items, we must have:
%   - The $260 lot is one of the four.
%   - The 4th lot is one of the four.
%   - The 7th lot is one of the four.
%   - The Roland lot is one of the four.
% And since there are only four items, each of these four attributes corresponds to a different item.
% Therefore, no two of these can be the same item.
% So: the item with price=260 is not the 4th lot, not the 7th lot, not won by Roland.
% But wait — that would mean the $260 item is not the 4th, not the 7th, not Roland → so it must be the 3rd.
% Similarly, the 4th lot is not $260, not 7th, not Roland → so it must be Daniel or Gabriel.
% But we can enforce this via constraints.

% Enforce that the four items are uniquely identified by the four properties.
% So: the item with price=260 is not the 4th lot, not the 7th lot, not won by Roland.
:- match(W, 260, O), O = 4.
:- match(W, 260, O), O = 7.
:- match("Roland", 260, O).

% The 4th lot is not the $260 lot, not the 7th lot, not won by Roland.
:- match(W, P, 4), P = 260.
:- match(W, P, 4), W = "Roland".

% The 7th lot is not the $260 lot, not won by Roland.
:- match(W, 260, 7).
:- match("Roland", P, 7).

% The Roland lot is not the $260 lot, not the 4th lot, not the 7th lot.
:- match("Roland", 260, O).
:- match("Roland", P, 4).
:- match("Roland", P, 7).

% The above constraints ensure that the four items are distinct and cover all four.
% Thus, the only possible order for the $260 lot is order=3.
% Similarly, the 4th lot must be won by Daniel or Gabriel, etc.
% But we don't need to hardcode — the constraints above are sufficient.

% Final: minimize the number of predicates — we only use one: match(W, P, O)
% This covers all categories and allows expressing all clues.
% Therefore, the minimal predicate set is just match(W, P, O).
```

```asp
% Define the constants in each category.
superheroes("Deep Shadow"; "Green Avenger"; "Max Fusion"; "Ultra Hex").
years(2007; 2008; 2009; 2010).
real_names("Arnold Ashley"; "Lyle Lucas"; "Orel Osborne"; "Red Reilly").

% Each person has exactly one superhero, one real name, and one start year.
% We model the assignment as a ternary relation: assigned(S, Y, R) meaning superhero S started in year Y and has real name R.
{assigned(S, Y, R): superheroes(S), years(Y), real_names(R)}=1 :- real_names(R).
{assigned(S, Y, R): superheroes(S), years(Y), real_names(R)}=1 :- years(Y).
{assigned(S, Y, R): superheroes(S), years(Y), real_names(R)}=1 :- superheroes(S).

% Clue 1: The four people are the person who started in 2007, Green Avenger, Deep Shadow, and Lyle Lucas.
% This implies that the set of individuals is fully described by these four entities, so we must have:
% - The person who started in 2007 is one of the four.
% - Green Avenger is one of the four.
% - Deep Shadow is one of the four.
% - Lyle Lucas is one of the four.
% But since each person has a unique real name, superhero, and year, this is already enforced by the one-to-one assignment.

% Clue 2: Arnold Ashley began 1 year after Ultra Hex.
% So: year(assigned(arnold_ashley)) = year(assigned(ultra_hex)) + 1
:- assigned("Ultra Hex", Y, _), assigned("Arnold Ashley", Y2, _), Y2 != Y + 1.

% Clue 3: The person who started in 2009 is Arnold Ashley.
:- assigned(_, 2009, R), R != "Arnold Ashley".

% Clue 4: Green Avenger is Orel Osborne.
:- assigned("Green Avenger", _, R), R != "Orel Osborne".

% Enforce that each superhero, year, and real name is used exactly once.
:- #count{S, Y, R : assigned(S, Y, R)} != 4.
:- #count{S, Y, R : assigned(S, Y, R)} != 4.
:- #count{S, Y, R : assigned(S, Y, R)} != 4.

% Ensure all categories are covered and no duplicates.
:- #count{R : assigned(_, _, R)} != 4.
:- #count{S : assigned(S, _, _)} != 4.
:- #count{Y : assigned(_, Y, _)} != 4.
```

```asp
% Define the constants in each category.
company("Omnipax"; "Rubicorp"; "SpaceZen"; "Ubersplore").
rocket("Beritron"; "Exatris"; "Foltron"; "Worul").
month(1; 2; 3; 4).

% Define the launch relation: company C launches rocket R in month M.
launch(C, R, M) :- company(C), rocket(R), month(M).

% Each company launches exactly one rocket in exactly one month.
{launch(C, R, M): rocket(R), month(M)}=1 :- company(C).

% Each rocket is launched by exactly one company in exactly one month.
{launch(C, R, M): company(C), month(M)}=1 :- rocket(R).

% Each month has exactly one launch.
{launch(C, R, M): company(C), rocket(R)}=1 :- month(M).

% Clue 1: The Exatris will launch 1 month after the Worul.
:- launch(C1, "Exatris", M1), launch(C2, "Worul", M2), M1 != M2 + 1.

% Clue 2: Of the Foltron and the rocket that will launch in January, one is made by Ubersplore and the other is made by Rubicorp.
% First, define which company made which rocket.
company_of(R, C) :- launch(C, R, M).

% For the rocket launched in January, determine its company.
rocket_in_january(R) :- launch(C, R, 1).

% Now, consider the two rockets: Foltron and the one launched in January.
% One is made by Ubersplore, the other by Rubicorp.
:- rocket_in_january(R), company_of("Foltron", C1), company_of(R, C2), 
   C1 != "Ubersplore", C2 != "Ubersplore", 
   C1 != "Rubicorp", C2 != "Rubicorp".

:- rocket_in_january(R), company_of("Foltron", C1), company_of(R, C2), 
   C1 == "Ubersplore", C2 == "Ubersplore".

:- rocket_in_january(R), company_of("Foltron", C1), company_of(R, C2), 
   C1 == "Rubicorp", C2 == "Rubicorp".
```

```asp
% Define the constants in each category.
borrower("Cory"; "Rosa"; "Sherrie"; "Vicki").
due_date(1; 8; 15; 22).
title("Dancing Well"; "Heaven's Seal"; "Stars Below"; "Time to Burn").

% Define the core relation: each title is borrowed by one borrower and has one due date.
% Each title must be matched to exactly one borrower and one due date.
{book(T, B, D): borrower(B), due_date(D)}=1 :- title(T).

% Clue 1: The four books are Rosa's book, the book due on 22, Dancing Well, and the book due on 15.
% This is implicitly satisfied by the universe of constants and the one-to-one matching.

% Clue 2: Heaven's Seal is either Vicki's book or the book due on September 8.
:- book("Heaven's Seal", B, D), B != "Vicki", D != 8.

% Clue 3: Of Heaven's Seal and the book due on 22, one was taken out by Cory and the other by Vicki.
% So the borrowers of these two books are Cory and Vicki, in some order.
% We need to ensure that:
%   - One of them is borrowed by Cory
%   - The other is borrowed by Vicki
%   - And they are different books.
:- book("Heaven's Seal", B1, D1), book(T2, B2, 22), B1 == B2.
:- book("Heaven's Seal", B1, D1), book(T2, B2, 22), B1 != "Cory", B1 != "Vicki".
:- book("Heaven's Seal", B1, D1), book(T2, B2, 22), B2 != "Cory", B2 != "Vicki".

% Clue 4: Sherrie's book was due 2 weeks before Stars Below.
% So: due_date(Sherrie's book) = due_date(Stars Below) - 2
:- book("Sherrie", B, D), book("Stars Below", B2, D2), D != D2 - 2.
```

```asp
% Define the constants in each category.
companies("Buralde"; "Denlend"; "Irycia"; "Kimsight").
pieces(250; 500; 750; 1000).
themes("autumn leaves"; "coral reef"; "outer space"; "rustic village").

% Each puzzle has one company, one piece count, and one theme.
% The predicate puzzle(C, P, T) represents a puzzle made by company C, with P pieces, and theme T.
puzzle(C, P, T) :- companies(C), pieces(P), themes(T).

% Ensure each company, piece count, and theme is used exactly once (bijection).
:- #count{P, T : puzzle(C, P, T)} != 1, companies(C).
:- #count{C, T : puzzle(C, P, T)} != 1, pieces(P).
:- #count{C, P : puzzle(C, P, T)} != 1, themes(T).

% Clue 1: The puzzle with the "autumn leaves" theme has somewhat more pieces than the puzzle with the "rustic village" theme.
puzzle(C1, P1, "autumn leaves"), puzzle(C2, P2, "rustic village") :- P1 > P2.

% Clue 2: The four puzzles are: Buralde, rustic village, autumn leaves, and 1000 pieces.
% This means: the puzzle made by Buralde, the one with rustic village theme, the one with autumn leaves theme, and the one with 1000 pieces are all distinct puzzles.
% So we must have:
% - Buralde exists
% - rustic village exists
% - autumn leaves exists
% - 1000 pieces exists
% And they are all different puzzles, so we don't need to add extra constraints beyond the bijection and the above.

% Clue 3: The puzzle made by Buralde has the "outer space" theme.
:- puzzle("Buralde", P, T), T != "outer space".

% Clue 4: The puzzle made by Denlend has somewhat fewer pieces than the puzzle with the "outer space" theme.
puzzle("Denlend", P1, _), puzzle(_, P2, "outer space") :- P1 < P2.

% Clue 5: The puzzle made by Kimsight has 250 more pieces than the puzzle made by Denlend.
puzzle("Kimsight", P1, _), puzzle("Denlend", P2, _), P1 = P2 + 250.
```

```asp
% Define the constants in each category.
height(144; 147; 150; 153).
tree("Evan's Fir"; "Old Jarvis"; "Nolan's Pine"; "Zeke's Spruce").
age(79; 80; 96; 99).

% For each tree, it has exactly one height and one age.
{tree_info(Tree, Height, Age): height(Height), age(Age)}=1 :- tree(Tree).
```

```asp
% Define the constants in each category.
owners("Anita"; "Elsie"; "Fernando"; "Ginger").
years(2006; 2007; 2008; 2009).
breeds("bulldog"; "dalmatian"; "irish setter"; "maltese").

% For each owner O, there is exactly one breed B and one year Y such that O won with B in Y.
{won(O, B, Y): breeds(B), years(Y)}=1 :- owners(O).
```

```asp
% Define the constants in each category.
employees("Andy"; "Brett"; "Victor"; "Zachary").
riders(50; 75; 100; 125).
section("blue"; "green"; "red"; "yellow").

% For each employee E, assign exactly 1 section S and 1 rider R.
{assign(E, S, R): section(S), riders(R)}=1 :- employees(E).
```

```asp
% Define the constants in each category.
players("Charles"; "Evan"; "Karl"; "Vincent").
games(8; 9; 10; 11).
positions("center field"; "first base"; "shortstop"; "third base").

% For each player, they have exactly one position and one number of games.
{player_info(Player, Position, Games): positions(Position), games(Games)}=1 :- players(Player).
```
